blurb: The rules in this category are known in Pali as nissaggiya pācittiya, “entailing relinquishment and confession”. There are thirty such rules for the monks, the same number as for the nuns, although only eighteen are held in common. These rules, together with the remaining rules of the Monastic Code, are classed as light offenses, lahukāpatti. For light offenses there is usually no penalty apart from confession. For the nissaggiya pācittiyas, however, any item involved in the committing of an offense must be relinquished. Once the offense has been confessed, the item is, with a few exceptions, returned to the offender.

children.translated_name: 1. The training rule on keeping extra robe-cloth beyond ten days
children.blurb: A monk who keeps extra robe-cloth for more than ten day must relinquish the robe-cloth and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 2. Staying apart from one’s robes
children.blurb: A monk who stays apart from any of his three robes for more than one day must relinquish the robe and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 3. Keeping extra robe-cloth beyond a month
children.blurb: A monk may keep extra robe-cloth for a month if he is expecting more. A monk who keeps it beyond that must relinquish the robe-cloth and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 4. Having a nun wash a used robe
children.blurb: A monk who makes a nun wash or dye a used robe must relinquish the robe and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 5. Receiving a robe from a nun
children.blurb: A monk who accepts robe-cloth from a nun must relinquish the robe-cloth and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 6. Asking an unrelated person for a robe
children.blurb: A monk who asks a lay person for robe-cloth, except if his robes are lost or destroyed, must relinquish the robe-cloth and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 7. Accepting too many robes
children.blurb: When a monk’s robes are lost or destroyed, he should show moderation in asking for robe-cloth. If he does not, he must relinquish the robe-cloth and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 8. Requesting a fine robe from a fund
children.blurb: If a lay person wants to buy a robe for a monk, and the monk asks them for a fine robe, he must relinquish the robe and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 9. Requesting a fine robe from a combined fund
children.blurb: If two separate lay people want to buy a robe for a monk, and the monk asks them to put their money together to buy him a fine robe, he must relinquish the robe and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 10. Excessive prompting to get a robe
children.blurb: When money to be used for a monk’s robe has been placed with a steward in the proper way, that monk may hint to the steward that he needs a robe. If he does not get a robe in this way, but he does get one by acting inappropriately, he must relinquish the robe and confess the offense. This rule also prescribes the procedure through which a lay follower comes to hold money on behalf of a donor who wishes to support a monk.

children.translated_name: 11. Making a blanket with silk
children.blurb: A monk who makes a blanket containing silk must relinquish it and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 12. Making a blanket entirely of black wool
children.blurb: A monk who makes a blanket entirely of black wool must relinquish it and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 13. Making a new blanket with too much black wool
children.blurb: A monk who makes a blanket that contains more than fifty percent black wool must relinquish it and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 14. Keeping a new blanket less than six years
children.blurb: A monk who makes a blanket must wait for six years before making another. If he does not, he must relinquish it and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 15. Making an inappropriate sitting blanket
children.blurb: A monk who makes a sitting-mat without including a piece from an old blanket must relinquish it and confess the offense. This rule starts with a narrative that includes several important principles of monastic law.

children.translated_name: 16. Carrying wool too far
children.blurb: A monk who carries wool on a long journey must relinquish it and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 17. Getting a nun to wash wool
children.blurb: A monk who makes a nun wash, dye, or comb unspun wool must relinquish it and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 18. Becoming the owner of money
children.blurb: A monk who receives money must relinquish it and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 19. Trading with money
children.blurb: A monk who receives money through trading must relinquish it and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 20. Bartering
children.blurb: A monk who barters, except with fellow monastics, must relinquish the item received and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 21. Keeping an extra almsbowl beyond ten days
children.blurb: A monk who keeps an extra bowl for more than ten days must relinquish it and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 22. Exchanging an almsbowl with fewer than five mends
children.blurb: A monk who asks for a new bowl when his existing bowl is still useable must relinquish it and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 23. Storing the five tonics too long
children.blurb: A monk who keeps tonics for more than seven days must relinquish them and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 24. Making a rainy-season robe at the wrong time
children.blurb: A monk who seeks or uses a rainy-season robe inappropriately must relinquish it and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 25. Taking back robe-cloth in anger
children.blurb: A monk who gives robe-cloth to another monk, but then takes it back in anger, must relinquish it and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 26. Asking for thread
children.blurb: A monk who asks for thread and then has robe-cloth woven with it must relinquish the robe-cloth and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 27. Bribing weavers
children.blurb: If a lay person is having robe-cloth woven for a monk and that monk makes demands of the weavers, then he must relinquish the robe-cloth and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 28. Storing haste-cloth too long
children.blurb: A monk who stores “an urgent robe” beyond the acceptable time limit must relinquish it and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 29. Storing a robe for more than six days in an inhabited area
children.blurb: A monk who stores any of his three robes in an inhabited area for more than six days must relinquish the robe and confess the offense.

children.translated_name: 30. Diverting an offering intended for the Sangha
children.blurb: A monk who diverts to himself an offering intended for the Order must relinquish the item and confess the offense.
